citations_appears_in_source.cited_case_name: Cook v. Bounds
citations_appears_in_source.parenthetical: noting that appointment of counsel "should be allowed only in exceptional cases"
citations_appears_in_source.sentence_context: Specifically, as to the request for counsel, the district court found Appellant had not identified any exceptional circumstances meriting the appointment of counsel in a civil action. See Cook v. Bounds, 518 F.2d 779, 780 (4th Cir. 1975) (noting that appointment of counsel "should be allowed only in exceptional cases").

citations_appears_in_source.cited_case_name: Zinkand v. Brown
citations_appears_in_source.sentence_context: On August 9, 2017, the district court denied Appellant's January 27 motion for reconsideration as moot and denied Appellant's amended motion for reconsideration because Appellant had failed to show any intervening change in law, new evidence, or that manifest injustice would result. See Zinkand v. Brown, 478 F.3d 634, 637 (4th Cir. 2007) (identifying proper grounds for reconsideration).

citations_appears_in_source.cited_case_name: Erickson v. Pardus
citations_appears_in_source.sentence_context: Because "[a] document filed pro se is to be liberally construed," Erickson v. Pardus, 551 U.S. 89, 94 (2007) (per curiam) (internal quotation marks omitted), we take Appellant's notice of appeal to also contest the January 17 dismissal of his action, not simply the judgment denying his amended motion for reconsideration.

citations_appears_in_source.cited_case_name: Grayson O Co. v. Agadir Int'l LLC
citations_appears_in_source.sentence_context: Appellant has not raised any issues related to the denial of this motion, thus any such issues are waived. See Grayson O Co. v. Agadir Int'l LLC, 856 F.3d 307, 316 (4th Cir. 2017).

citations_appears_in_source.cited_case_name: United States ex rel. Carter v. Halliburton Co.
citations_appears_in_source.sentence_context: The FCA prohibits "knowingly presenting false or fraudulent claims to the government of the United States for payment or approval." United States ex rel. Carter v. Halliburton Co., 866 F.3d 199, 202 (4th Cir. 2017) (citing 31 U.S.C. § 3729(a)(1)).

citations_appears_in_source.cited_case_name: Vt. Agency of Nat. Res. v. United States ex rel. Stevens
citations_appears_in_source.sentence_context: As the Supreme Court has explained, "The FCA can reasonably be regarded as effecting a partial assignment of the Government's damages claim" to the relator. Vt. Agency of Nat. Res. v. United States ex rel. Stevens, 529 U.S. 765, 773 (2000).

citations_appears_in_source.cited_case_name: Pridgen v. Andresen
citations_appears_in_source.parenthetical: [A]ppearance pro se denotes . . . appearance for one's self; so that a person ordinarily may not appear pro se in the cause of another person or entity.
citations_appears_in_source.sentence_context: see also Pridgen v. Andresen, 113 F.3d 391, 393 (2d Cir. 1997) ("[A]ppearance pro se denotes . . . appearance for one's self; so that a person ordinarily may not appear pro se in the cause of another person or entity.").

citations_appears_in_source.cited_case_name: Winkelman ex rel. Winkelman v. Parma City Sch. Dist.
citations_appears_in_source.sentence_context: "[T]here is no question that a party may represent his or her own interests in federal court without the aid of counsel." Winkelman ex rel. Winkelman v. Parma City Sch. Dist., 550 U.S. 516, 522 (2007) (citing 28 U.S.C. § 1654).

citations_appears_in_source.cited_case_name: Myers v. Loudoun Cty. Pub. Sch.
citations_appears_in_source.sentence_context: "The right to litigate for oneself, however, does not create a coordinate right to litigate for others." Myers v. Loudoun Cty. Pub. Sch., 418 F.3d 395, 400 (4th Cir. 2005) (emphases in original) (citation omitted).

citations_appears_in_source.cited_case_name: Marex Titanic, Inc. v. Wrecked and Abandoned Vessel
citations_appears_in_source.sentence_context: Reviewing the district court's Rule 41 dismissal de novo, see Marex Titanic, Inc. v. Wrecked and Abandoned Vessel, 2 F.3d 544, 545 (4th Cir. 1993), we agree that a relator cannot pursue a qui tam FCA suit pro se.

citations_appears_in_source.cited_case_name: Vt. Agency of Nat. Res. v. United States ex rel. Stevens
citations_appears_in_source.sentence_context: Though the FCA's "partial assignment" of a claim "gives the relator himself an interest in the lawsuit," that right to recovery is inextricably bound up with the Government's interest. Vt. Agency of Nat. Res. v. United States ex rel. Stevens, 529 U.S. 765, 772–73 (2000) (emphasis omitted).

citations_appears_in_source.cited_case_name: Stoner v. Santa Clara Cty. Office of Educ.
citations_appears_in_source.sentence_context: Even if the United States does not intervene in a qui tam action brought pursuant to the FCA, "the United States is bound by the relator's actions for purposes of res judicata and collateral estoppel." Stoner v. Santa Clara Cty. Office of Educ., 502 F.3d 1116, 1126 (9th Cir. 2007) (internal quotation marks omitted); see also Oxendine v. Williams, 509 F.2d 1405, 1407 (4th Cir. 1975) (per curiam) (noting that allowing pro se inmate to represent class of inmates risks a negative judgment that "may prevent the other inmates from later raising the same claims" and concluding pro se inmate could not represent class).

citations_appears_in_source.cited_case_name: Oxendine v. Williams
citations_appears_in_source.sentence_context: see also Oxendine v. Williams, 509 F.2d 1405, 1407 (4th Cir. 1975) (per curiam) (noting that allowing pro se inmate to represent class of inmates risks a negative judgment that "may prevent the other inmates from later raising the same claims" and concluding pro se inmate could not represent class).

citations_appears_in_source.cited_case_name: Myers v. Loudoun Cty. Pub. Sch.
citations_appears_in_source.parenthetical: holding "that non-attorney parents generally may not litigate the claims of their minor children in federal court"
citations_appears_in_source.sentence_context: See Myers v. Loudoun Cty. Pub. Sch., 418 F.3d 395, 401 (4th Cir. 2005) (holding "that non-attorney parents generally may not litigate the claims of their minor children in federal court").

citations_appears_in_source.cited_case_name: Cheung v. Youth Orchestra Found. of Buffalo, Inc.
citations_appears_in_source.sentence_context: Allowing individuals to represent themselves pro se "reflects a respect for the choice of an individual citizen to plead his or her own cause," but so does the bar preventing individuals without legal expertise from representing others. Id. (quoting Cheung v. Youth Orchestra Found. of Buffalo, Inc., 906 F.2d 59, 61 (2d Cir. 1990)); see Olagues v. Timken, 908 F.3d 200, 203 (6th Cir. 2018) ("[W]hile a pro se plaintiff can squander his own rights, he cannot waste the rights of other persons or entities[.]" (internal quotation marks omitted)).

citations_appears_in_source.cited_case_name: Olagues v. Timken
citations_appears_in_source.parenthetical: [W]hile a pro se plaintiff can squander his own rights, he cannot waste the rights of other persons or entities[.]
citations_appears_in_source.sentence_context: see Olagues v. Timken, 908 F.3d 200, 203 (6th Cir. 2018) ("[W]hile a pro se plaintiff can squander his own rights, he cannot waste the rights of other persons or entities[.]" (internal quotation marks omitted)).

citations_appears_in_source.cited_case_name: Oxendine
citations_appears_in_source.parenthetical: construing prisoner's request for injunctive relief against prison policy impacting all inmates as a class action
citations_appears_in_source.sentence_context: Applying this reasoning, we have held, for example, that a pro se prisoner may not bring a class action on behalf of other prisoners because "we consider the competence of a layman representing himself to be clearly too limited to allow him to risk the rights of others." Oxendine, 509 F.2d at 1407 (construing prisoner's request for injunctive relief against prison policy impacting all inmates as a class action).

citations_appears_in_source.cited_case_name: Murray ex rel. Purnell v. City of Philadelphia
citations_appears_in_source.parenthetical: If an estate has one or more beneficiaries besides the administrator, then the case is not the administrator's own because the interests of other parties are directly at stake
citations_appears_in_source.sentence_context: See, e.g., Murray ex rel. Purnell v. City of Philadelphia, 901 F.3d 169, 171 (3d Cir. 2018) ("If an estate has one or more beneficiaries besides the administrator, then the case is not the administrator's own because the interests of other parties are directly at stake" and those interests "may not be represented by a non-attorney administrator of an estate.").

citations_appears_in_source.cited_case_name: Georgakis v. Ill. State Univ.
citations_appears_in_source.parenthetical: [T]o maintain a suit on behalf of the government, the relator (as the qui tam plaintiff is termed) has to be either licensed as a lawyer or represented by a lawyer . . . . A nonlawyer can't handle a case on behalf of anyone except himself.
citations_appears_in_source.sentence_context: See, e.g., Georgakis v. Ill. State Univ., 722 F.3d 1075, 1077 (7th Cir. 2013) ("[T]o maintain a suit on behalf of the government, the relator (as the qui tam plaintiff is termed) has to be either licensed as a lawyer or represented by a lawyer . . . . A nonlawyer can't handle a case on behalf of anyone except himself." (citations omitted));

citations_appears_in_source.cited_case_name: Timson v. Sampson
citations_appears_in_source.sentence_context: Timson v. Sampson, 518 F.3d 870, 873 (11th Cir. 2008) (per curiam) (holding a plaintiff may not bring a qui tam FCA action as a pro se relator);

citations_appears_in_source.cited_case_name: United States ex rel. Mergent Servs. v. Flaherty
citations_appears_in_source.parenthetical: Because relators lack a personal interest in False Claims Act qui tam actions, we conclude that they are not entitled to proceed pro se.
citations_appears_in_source.sentence_context: United States ex rel. Mergent Servs. v. Flaherty, 540 F.3d 89, 93 (2d Cir. 2008) ("Because relators lack a personal interest in False Claims Act qui tam actions, we conclude that they are not entitled to proceed pro se.");

citations_appears_in_source.cited_case_name: Stoner v. Santa Clara Cty. Office of Educ.
citations_appears_in_source.sentence_context: Stoner v. Santa Clara Cty. Office of Educ., 502 F.3d 1116, 1126–28 (9th Cir. 2007); United States v. Onan, 190 F.2d 1, 6–7 (8th Cir. 1951).

citations_appears_in_source.cited_case_name: United States v. Onan
citations_appears_in_source.sentence_context: Stoner v. Santa Clara Cty. Office of Educ., 502 F.3d 1116, 1126–28 (9th Cir. 2007); United States v. Onan, 190 F.2d 1, 6–7 (8th Cir. 1951).

citations_appears_in_source.cited_case_name: Nasuti v. Savage Farms, Inc.
citations_appears_in_source.sentence_context: Nasuti v. Savage Farms, Inc., No. 14-1362, 2015 WL 9598315, at *1 (1st Cir. Mar. 12, 2015);

citations_appears_in_source.cited_case_name: Onan
citations_appears_in_source.parenthetical: explaining, where Congress did not expressly authorize relator to proceed pro se, it "must have had in mind that such a suit would be carried on in accordance with the established procedure which requires that only one licensed to practice law may conduct proceedings in court for anyone other than himself"
citations_appears_in_source.sentence_context: But there is no indication that this assignment was intended to abrogate the general rule that a person may not represent another person or entity pro se. See, e.g., Onan, 190 F.2d at 6 (explaining, where Congress did not expressly authorize relator to proceed pro se, it "must have had in mind that such a suit would be carried on in accordance with the established procedure which requires that only one licensed to practice law may conduct proceedings in court for anyone other than himself").

citations_appears_in_source.cited_case_name: United States ex rel. Carter v. Halliburton Co.
citations_appears_in_source.sentence_context: We review the denial of a motion for reconsideration under the deferential abuse of discretion standard. United States ex rel. Carter v. Halliburton Co., 866 F.3d 199, 206 (4th Cir. 2017) (citation omitted).

citations_appears_in_source.cited_case_name: Zinkand v. Brown
citations_appears_in_source.sentence_context: "Rule 59(e) motions [for reconsideration] can be successful in only three situations: (1) to accommodate an intervening change in controlling law; (2) to account for new evidence . . . ; or (3) to correct a clear error of law or prevent manifest injustice." Zinkand v. Brown, 478 F.3d 634, 637 (4th Cir. 2007) (internal quotation marks omitted).

citations_appears_in_source.cited_case_name: Halliburton Co.
citations_appears_in_source.sentence_context: However, we have previously held that where the district court's initial decision was correct, the denial of a motion to reconsider cannot be clearly erroneous or manifestly unjust. See Halliburton Co., 866 F.3d at 211.

citations_appears_in_source.cited_case_name: U.S. ex rel. Sweigert v. Elec. Sys. Assocs., Inc.
citations_appears_in_source.sentence_context: U.S. ex rel. Sweigert v. Elec. Sys. Assocs., Inc., 85 F.3d 630, 630 (Table) (6th Cir. 1996) (affirming dismissal in appeal filed pro se without addressing lack of counsel);

citations_appears_in_source.cited_case_name: U.S. ex rel. Petrofsky v. Van Cott, Bagley, Cornwall, McCarthy
citations_appears_in_source.sentence_context: U.S. ex rel. Petrofsky v. Van Cott, Bagley, Cornwall, McCarthy, 588 F.2d 1327, 1328–29 (10th Cir. 1978) (per curiam) (affirming dismissal of qui tam suit brought pro se without considering effect of pro se status).

citations_near_miss_after_normalize.cited_case_name: United States ex rel. Carson v. Manor Care, Inc.
citations_near_miss_after_normalize.parenthetical: If a court finds that the particular action before it is barred by the first-to-file rule, the court lacks subject matter jurisdiction over the later-filed matter.
citations_near_miss_after_normalize.sentence_context: see also United States ex rel. Carson v. Manor Care, Inc., 851 F.3d 293, 303 (4th Cir. 2017) ("If a court finds that the particular action before it is barred by the first-to-file rule, the court lacks subject matter jurisdiction over the later-filed matter." (citation omitted)).

citations_near_miss_after_normalize.cited_case_name: U.S. ex rel. Brooks v. Lockheed Martin Corp.
citations_near_miss_after_normalize.sentence_context: However, we have recognized in an unpublished decision that, "[a] lay person may not bring a qui tam action under the False Claims Act." U.S. ex rel. Brooks v. Lockheed Martin Corp., 237 F. App'x 802, 803 (4th Cir. 2007) (per curiam).

More recently, we have applied this rule in another unpublished decision -- "Count II of [Appellant's] complaint was subject to dismissal because a pro se litigant may not pursue a qui tam action on behalf of the Government under the FCA." Bond v. Hughes, 671 F. App'x 228, 229 (4th Cir. 2016) (per curiam) (collecting cases).

citations_near_miss_after_normalize.cited_case_name: Gunn v. Credit Suisse Grp. AG
citations_near_miss_after_normalize.sentence_context: See also Gunn v. Credit Suisse Grp. AG, 610 F. App'x 155, 157 (3d Cir. 2015) (per curiam) ("[W]e do not hesitate to conclude that the District Court did not err in concluding that [Appellant] Gunn may not maintain this qui tam action in his pro se capacity as a relator on behalf of the United States.");

Jones v. Jindal, 409 F. App'x 356, 356 (D.C. Cir. 2011) (per curiam) ("The district court properly dismissed appellant's complaint because a pro se plaintiff may not file a qui tam action pursuant to the False Claims Act, 31 U.S.C. § 3729, et seq.").

citations_near_miss_after_normalize.cited_case_name: U.S. ex rel. Heath v. Dallas-Fort Worth Intern. Airport Bd.
citations_near_miss_after_normalize.sentence_context: See U.S. ex rel. Heath v. Dallas-Fort Worth Intern. Airport Bd., 260 F. App'x 708, 711 (5th Cir. 2005) (per curiam) (deciding pro se appeal from qui tam trial without noting lack of counsel issue);

citations_all.sentence_context: U.S. ex rel. Sweigert v. Elec. Sys. Assocs., Inc., 85 F.3d 630, 630 (Table) (6th Cir. 1996) (affirming dismissal in appeal filed pro se without addressing lack of counsel);

citations_all.cited_case_name: U.S. ex rel. Petrofsky v. Van Cott, Bagley, Cornwall, McCarthy
citations_all.sentence_context: U.S. ex rel. Petrofsky v. Van Cott, Bagley, Cornwall, McCarthy, 588 F.2d 1327, 1328–29 (10th Cir. 1978) (per curiam) (affirming dismissal of qui tam suit brought pro se without considering effect of pro se status).

citations_all.cited_case_name: United States ex rel. Carson v. Manor Care, Inc.
citations_all.parenthetical: If a court finds that the particular action before it is barred by the first-to-file rule, the court lacks subject matter jurisdiction over the later-filed matter.
citations_all.sentence_context: see also United States ex rel. Carson v. Manor Care, Inc., 851 F.3d 293, 303 (4th Cir. 2017) ("If a court finds that the particular action before it is barred by the first-to-file rule, the court lacks subject matter jurisdiction over the later-filed matter." (citation omitted)).

citations_all.cited_case_name: U.S. ex rel. Brooks v. Lockheed Martin Corp.
citations_all.sentence_context: However, we have recognized in an unpublished decision that, "[a] lay person may not bring a qui tam action under the False Claims Act." U.S. ex rel. Brooks v. Lockheed Martin Corp., 237 F. App'x 802, 803 (4th Cir. 2007) (per curiam).

citations_all.cited_case_name: Bond v. Hughes
citations_all.sentence_context: More recently, we have applied this rule in another unpublished decision -- "Count II of [Appellant's] complaint was subject to dismissal because a pro se litigant may not pursue a qui tam action on behalf of the Government under the FCA." Bond v. Hughes, 671 F. App'x 228, 229 (4th Cir. 2016) (per curiam) (collecting cases).

citations_all.cited_case_name: Gunn v. Credit Suisse Grp. AG
citations_all.sentence_context: See also Gunn v. Credit Suisse Grp. AG, 610 F. App'x 155, 157 (3d Cir. 2015) (per curiam) ("[W]e do not hesitate to conclude that the District Court did not err in concluding that [Appellant] Gunn may not maintain this qui tam action in his pro se capacity as a relator on behalf of the United States.");

citations_all.cited_case_name: Jones v. Jindal
citations_all.sentence_context: Jones v. Jindal, 409 F. App'x 356, 356 (D.C. Cir. 2011) (per curiam) ("The district court properly dismissed appellant's complaint because a pro se plaintiff may not file a qui tam action pursuant to the False Claims Act, 31 U.S.C. § 3729, et seq.").

citations_all.cited_case_name: U.S. ex rel. Heath v. Dallas-Fort Worth Intern. Airport Bd.
citations_all.sentence_context: See U.S. ex rel. Heath v. Dallas-Fort Worth Intern. Airport Bd., 260 F. App'x 708, 711 (5th Cir. 2005) (per curiam) (deciding pro se appeal from qui tam trial without noting lack of counsel issue);
